conditionals-1-2.title: Unit 21: Conditionals (If Clauses)
conditionals-1-2.content: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses) Conditional sentences are "If X, then Y" statements. In Greek, the word for "If" is Αν (An). There are three main ways to build these, depending on how likely the situation is. 1. Type 1: The Real (Possible) Use this for facts, plans, or likely events. Just use the normal Present or Future tenses. Formula: Αν [Present/Future], then [Future/Command] Αν βρέχει , θα μείνω σπίτι. (If it rains, I will stay home). Αν θέλεις , έλα μαζί μου. (If you want, come with me). 2. Type 2: The Unreal (Dreaming) Use this for hypotheticals, dreams, or giving advice. Greek pushes both verbs into the Continuous Past (Imperfect) . Formula: Αν [Past], then Θα + [Past] Αν είχα λεφτά, θα αγόραζα ένα πλοίο. (If I had money [now], I would buy a boat). Αν ήμουν εσύ, θα του έλεγα "ναι". (If I were you, I would say yes). 3. Type 3: The Regret (Past Unreal) Use this for things that never happened in the past. It’s too late! Formula: Αν [Past Perfect], then Θα + [Past Perfect] Αν είχα δει εσένα, θα είχα πει γεια. (If I had seen you, I would have said hello). Αν είχε διαβάσει , θα είχε περάσει. (If he had read, he would have passed). 🎯 Unit 21 Mastery Checklist I know that Αν means "If." I understand that for "Would," Greek uses Θα + Past Tense . I can build a hypothetical dream sentence using the Imperfect tense. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences Up Next: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Interjections

deponent-verbs.title: Unit 18: Passive & Deponent Verbs
deponent-verbs.content: Unit 18: Passive Voice & Deponent Verbs (-ομαι) Welcome to Type C verbs. These verbs don’t end in -ω ; they end in -ομαι (o-me) . This ending system is used for two very different things in Greek: the Passive Voice and Deponent Verbs . 1. What are Deponent Verbs? Some of the most common verbs in Greek look passive but are actually active . These are called Deponent Verbs. They only exist in the -ομαι form. Verb Meaning Example έρχομαι I come Από πού έρχεσαι ; γίνομαι I become / I happen Τι γίνεσαι ; (How are you?) χρειάζομαι I need Χρειάζομαι βοήθεια. σκέφτομαι I think Τι σκέφτεσαι ; 2. Present Tense Conjugation Both Passive and Deponent verbs use these "Type C" endings: Pronoun Ending Example: έρχομαι (Εγώ) -ομαι έρχ ομαι (Εσύ) -εσαι έρχ εσαι (Αυτό/ή/ό) -εται έρχ εται (Εμείς) -όμαστε ερχ όμαστε (Εσείς) -εστε έρχ εστε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ονται έρχ ονται TIP The Accent Shift : Notice that in the "We" form ( ερχόμαστε ), the accent mark jumps forward. This is a very common rhythmic rule in Greek! 3. The True Passive Voice You can also take an active verb (like πλένω - I wash) and flip it to the passive form ( πλένομαι - I am washed / I wash myself). Active : Πλένω το αυτοκίνητο. (I wash the car). Passive : Πλένομαι . (I am washing myself / I am taking a bath). "By Someone" (Από) To say "by" in a passive sentence, use από + Accusative. Το βιβλίο διαβάζεται από τον Γιώργο. (The book is read by George). 4. Past Passive (Aorist) The past tense of many passive verbs is marked by the letter Θ (Theta) and the endings -ηκα, -ηκες, -ηκε . Active Past : Έπλυνα (I washed). Passive Past : Πλύθηκα (I was washed / I bathed). CAUTION Not Always Theta! While -θηκα is common, many everyday verbs change their stem to use στ, φτ, or χτ before the -ηκα ending. For example: - ετοιμάζω ➔ ετοιμάστηκα (I got ready) - κρύβω ➔ κρύφτηκα (I hid myself) 🎯 Unit 18 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate έρχομαι in the present tense. I know that verbs ending in -ομαι can be either Active (Deponent) or Passive/Reflexive. I recognize the -ηκα ending as a marker of the Past Passive, even if the consonant before it changes. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Deponent Verbs & States Up Next: Unit 19: Imperfect vs. Aorist (Refined Comparison) (Note: We will further refine the past tense split in the next phase!)

diminutives.title: Unit 29: Diminutives
diminutives.content: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά) Greek is a language of intense emotion. You don't just drink a coffee; you drink a little coffee with a friend. You don't just see a dog; you see a huge, monstrous dog . To express these feelings of smallness, affection, or enormous size, Greek uses special suffixes attached to the end of nouns. 1. The Diminutives (Making things small or cute) Diminutives are incredibly common in spoken Greek. They are used to make things sound smaller, cuter, more polite, or more affectionate. When you order food, you rarely order a "τυρόπιτα" (cheese pie). You order a "τυροπιτάκι" (little cheese pie), because it sounds friendlier! Suffix 1: -άκι (The universal tiny-maker) This is by far the most common diminutive. It can be added to almost any noun, and it always turns the word into a Neuter noun (Το). Το σπίτι (the house) ➔ Το σπιτ άκι (the little house) Το παιδί (the child) ➔ Το παιδ άκι (the little child) Η τυρόπιτα (the cheese pie) ➔ Το τυροπιτ άκι (the little cheese pie) Ο καφές (the coffee) ➔ Το καφεδ άκι (the little coffee) (Notice how even masculine and feminine nouns become neuter when you add -άκι!) Suffix 2: -ίτσα / -ούλα (For Feminine Nouns) If you want to keep a word feminine while making it cute, you can use these suffixes. Η πόρτα (the door) ➔ Η πορτ ίτσα (the little door) Η αγάπη (the love) ➔ Η αγαπ ούλα μου (my sweet little love) Η Ελένη (Eleni) ➔ Η Ελεν ίτσα (Little Eleni) Suffix 3: -ούλης / -άκος (For Masculine Nouns) If you want to make a masculine person or animal sound cute or endearing. Ο παππούς (the grandfather) ➔ Ο παππ ούλης (the sweet old grandpa) Ο Γιώργος (George) ➔ Ο Γιωργ άκος (Little George) 2. The Augmentatives (Making things HUGE) Sometimes you need to emphasize that something is massive, impressive, or even a bit scary. For this, Greek uses Augmentative suffixes. These suffixes always turn the noun into a Masculine noun (Ο), or sometimes Feminine (Η) for certain words. Suffix 1: -αρος (The Giant) This is the most common masculine augmentative. Το σκυλί (the dog) ➔ Ο σκύλ αρος (the massive beast of a dog) Το αυτοκίνητο (the car) ➔ Ο αυτοκινητ άρας (the huge, impressive car) Ο άντρας (the man) ➔ Ο αντρ αρος (the big, burly man) Suffix 2: -κλα (The Huge Feminine) This suffix creates a sense of enormous, sometimes overwhelming size. Η πόρτα (the door) ➔ Η πορτά κλα (the gigantic door) Η φωνή (the voice) ➔ Η φωνά κλα (the huge, booming voice) 3. The Power of Emotion Remember, these suffixes aren't just about physical size. They are about emotion . If a friend invites you for a <καφεδάκι> (little coffee), they aren't talking about a small cup. They are talking about a relaxed, friendly, intimate chat. If someone calls their new car an <αυτοκινητάρας> , they are expressing pride and awe. Mastering these suffixes is the key to sounding like a true native speaker! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist I know that -άκι makes words small/cute, and always turns them into Το (Neuter). I know the feminine diminutives -ίτσα and -ούλα . I know the masculine diminutives -ούλης and -άκος . I know that -αρος and -κλα are used to make things sound completely massive!

exercise-actions.title: Unit 4-5: Accusative Action
exercise-actions.content: Unit 4-5: Action & The Accusative This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (A rainy street in Thessaloniki, Greece. People holding umbrellas and hurrying) Power Lexis: Η βροχή (i vro-chí) - The rain Η ομπρέλα (i om-bré-la) - The umbrella Κρατάω (kra-táo) - I hold Τρέχω (tré-kho) - I run Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Use one Type A action verb (e.g. κρατάω, τρέχω). 2. Include a direct object in the Accusative case . Pro Tip: Remember that feminine nouns keep the "α" or "η" in the accusative, but the definite article changes from "η" to "την". 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Βρέχει πολύ σήμερα. Ο άνθρωπος κρατάει την ομπρέλα.» (It is raining a lot today. The man is holding the umbrella.) Why it works: κρατάει την ομπρέλα : "Κρατάει" is the 3rd person singular of the Type A action verb "κρατάω". "Την ομπρέλα" is the direct object receiving the action, correctly inflected in the accusative case (changing from "Η ομπρέλα" to "Την ομπρέλα"). 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base: Διαβάζω το βιβλίο. (I read the book.) Trigger: Change subject to Ο δάσκαλος . Target: Ο δάσκαλος διαβάζει το βιβλίο. Base: Κρατάει την ομπρέλα. (He/She holds the umbrella.) Trigger: Change subject to Εγώ (I). Target: Κρατάω την ομπρέλα. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of the Accusative Case and Type A verbs. «Βλέπω την τσάντα. Η γυναίκα κρατάει την τσάντα. Βλέπω τον καφέ. Ο άντρας πίνει τον καφέ. Εγώ έχω το νερό. Πίνω το νερό και βλέπω τον ουρανό. Κάνω μία βόλτα.»

exercise-adjectives.title: Unit 6-7: Adjectives & Objects
exercise-adjectives.content: Unit 6-7: Adjectives & Questions This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (Two people looking at colorful fresh fruit at an outdoor Greek market under the sunlight) Power Lexis: Φρέσκος, -η, -ο (frés-kos) - Fresh Κόκκινος, -η, -ο (kó-ki-nos) - Red Πόσο (pó-so) - How much Κάνει (ká-ni) - It costs (literally "it makes") Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Ask a question using the essential phrase Πόσο κάνει; 2. Use an adjective agreeing in gender with its noun. Pro Tip: In Greek, adjectives must always match the gender (masculine, feminine, or neuter), number, and case of the noun they describe. 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Αυτό το φρούτο είναι κόκκινο και φρέσκο. Πόσο κάνει;» (This fruit is red and fresh. How much does it cost?) Why it works: κόκκινο και φρέσκο : The noun "το φρούτο" is neuter. Therefore, the adjectives "κόκκινος" and "φρέσκος" must drop their masculine "-ος" ending and take the neuter "-ο" ending to become "κόκκινο" and "φρέσκο". Πόσο κάνει; : This is the standard, everyday way to ask for the price of a singular item in Greece. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base: Το κόκκινο μήλο κάνει δύο ευρώ. (The red apple costs two euros.) Trigger: Change subject to Ο καφές (The coffee - Masculine). Target: Ο κόκκινος καφές κάνει δύο ευρώ. Base: Πόσο κάνει η μικρή τσάντα; (How much does the small bag cost?) Trigger: Change subject to Το βιβλίο (The book - Neuter). Target: Πόσο κάνει το μικρό βιβλίο; 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of adjectives and basic questions. «Το μεγάλο σπίτι έχει μία κόκκινη πόρτα. Η μικρή πόρτα είναι ανοιχτή. Ποιος είναι εκεί; Ένας καλός φίλος είναι εκεί. Τι θέλει; Θέλει λίγο κρύο νερό. Πόσο κάνει το κρύο νερό; Είναι δωρεάν!»

exercise-basics.title: Unit 1-3: The Basics
exercise-basics.content: Unit 1-3: Foundations This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (An old Greek grandmother cooking in a rustic, sunlight-filled kitchen) Power Lexis: Η γιαγιά (i ya-yá) - The grandmother Το φαγητό (to fa-yi-tó) - The food Είναι (í-ne) - He/She/It is Έχω (é-kho) - I have Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Describe the scene using the verb "to be" (είμαι). 2. Describe an action/possession using the verb "to have" (έχω). Pro Tip: Keep it simple! Focus on creating short, complete sentences that use the definite article correctly. 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχει ωραίο φαγητό.» (The grandmother is in the kitchen. She has nice food.) Why it works: Η γιαγιά είναι... : Correct use of the feminine definite article "η" with the subject noun and the 3rd person singular present tense form of "είμαι". Έχει ωραίο φαγητό : The verb "έχω" is conjugated to the 3rd person singular ("έχει" - she has). "Ωραίο φαγητό" (nice food) follows as the related object of possession. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base: Εγώ είμαι στην κουζίνα. (I am in the kitchen.) Trigger: Change subject to Η γιαγιά . Target: Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Base: Έχω ωραίο φαγητό. (I have nice food.) Trigger: Change subject to Ο πατέρας . Target: Ο πατέρας έχει ωραίο φαγητό. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of to be and to have . «Το σπίτι είναι μεγάλο. Η γιαγιά είναι στην κουζίνα. Έχει ωραίο φαγητό. Το παιδί είναι εδώ. Έχει ένα βιβλίο. Το βιβλίο είναι καλό. Η μητέρα είναι και αυτή εδώ. Είναι όλοι καλά!»

exercise-plurals.title: Unit 8-10: Plurals & Verbs
exercise-plurals.content: Unit 8-10: Plurals & Verb Groups This exercise is divided into four fluency-building activities to move you from theory to automaticity. 1. The Constrained Picture Exercise (A stylish woman trying on different coats in a high-end boutique in Athens) Power Lexis: Το παλτό (to pal-tó) - The coat -> Τα παλτά (ta pal-tá) Μικρός, -ή, -ό (mi-krós) - Small -> Μικρά (mi-krá) Φοράω (fo-ráo) - I wear Ακριβός, -ή, -ό (a-kri-vós) - Expensive Grammar Tags (Goals): 1. Use Plural Nouns and Adjectives . 2. Conjgate a Group B verb (e.g. φοράω). Pro Tip: Neuter nouns ending in -ο or stressed -ό in the singular change to -α or stressed -ά in the plural. Adjectives follow the exact same pattern! 2. Audio-Visual Shadowing Listen to the native speaker describing the scene. Your goal is to speak at the exact same time , mimicking the rhythm and speed. Your browser does not support the audio element. Click to reveal Transcript «Η γυναίκα φοράει τα μικρά παλτά. Είναι πολύ ακριβά!» (The woman is wearing the small coats. They are very expensive!) Why it works: τα μικρά παλτά / πολύ ακριβά : "Το παλτό" becomes "Τα παλτά" in the plural. Accordingly, the adjectives "μικρό" and "ακριβό" change their endings to match the neuter plural form (μικρά, ακριβά). φοράει : "Φοράω" belongs to Verb Group B1 (verbs ending in an unstressed -άω in the "I" form). The "she" form takes the -άει ending. 3. The Mutation Drill (Speed-Shift) Read the "Base" sentence aloud. Then, look at the "Trigger" and instantly change the sentence mentally before checking the Target. This builds agility without overthinking. Base: Η γυναίκα φοράει το παλτό. (The woman wears the coat.) Trigger: Change object to Plural . Target: Η γυναίκα φοράει τα παλτά. Base: Το μικρό παιδί παίζει. (The small child plays.) Trigger: Change subject to Plural (and adjust verb). Target: Τα μικρά παιδιά παίζουν. 4. The Grammar-Saturated Story Read this short story three times: first for accuracy, second for comprehension, and third as fast as possible . This forces your brain to "soak" in the patterns of Plural Nouns and Group B Verbs. «Τα μαγαζιά είναι ακριβά. Οι γυναίκες αγαπούν τα ωραία ρούχα. Δοκιμάζουν τα μικρά φορέματα και τα μεγάλα παλτά. Οι πωλητές μιλούν πολύ. Τα ρούχα κοστίζουν πολλά ευρώ. Όλοι γελάνε.»

future-continuous.title: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα)
future-continuous.content: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα) In Unit 10, you learned the magic of Aspect . Now, we apply that "Video vs. Photo" logic to the future. In Greek, we don't just say "I will play"; we specify if it's a habit or a one-time thing. 1. The Future Particle: Θα Just like να and δεν , the word θα sits right in front of the verb. It translates to the English "will." English : I will play. Greek : Θα [verb]. 2. Continuous Future (The Video) If you want to talk about a habit, a routine, or something you will be doing repeatedly in the future, use the Continuous Stem (the Present tense stem). Formula: Θα + Present Tense Verb Θα διαβάζω κάθε μέρα. (I will [be reading] every day.) Θα έχουμε χρόνο. (We will have [ongoing] time.) 3. Simple Future (The Photo) If you are talking about a single, specific event that will happen and finish, use the Simple Stem (Aorist Stem). Formula: Θα + Simple Stem + Present Endings Θα διαβάσω το βιβλίο αύριο. (I will read the book tomorrow— once ). Θα παίξουμε τένις. (We will play tennis— one game ). CAUTION No Past Markings! Even though we use the Simple (Aorist) stem, do NOT add the past tense augment (the prefix ε- ) to the future. It’s θα γράψω , never θα έγραψα . 4. The Negation Combo To say "I will not," put δεν before θα . Δεν θα παίζω (I won't be playing regularly). Δεν θα παίξω (I won't play this specific time). 🎯 Unit 11 Mastery Checklist I can choose between θα παίζω (habit) and θα παίξω (one-off). I know how to negate the future with δεν θα . I understand that the future uses the present tense endings (-ω, -εις, -ει...). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Time & Calendar Up Next: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Aorist)

genitive-case.title: Unit 15: Genitive Case
genitive-case.content: Unit 15: The Genitive Case (Possession & Relationships) You’ve mastered Subjects (Nominative) and Objects (Accusative). Now, we enter the world of Ownership . The Genitive case is the Greek equivalent of the English apostrophe "s" ('s) or the word "of." 1. The Genitive Articles When a person or thing becomes an "Owner," the article must change. There is a very consistent pattern here: Gender Singular Owner Plural Owners Masculine του των Feminine της των Neuter του των TIP The Universal Plural In the Genitive plural, gender doesn't exist! Every single plural owner uses the article των and ends in -ων . - Των φίλων, των δασκάλων, των παιδιών. 2. Noun Endings (Singular) How does the noun itself change? Masculine (-ος, -ας, -ης) : Drops the final -ς . (e.g., ο πατέρας ➔ του πατέρα ). Nouns in -ος change to -ου. Feminine (-η, -α) : Always adds an -ς . (e.g., η Μαρία ➔ της Μαρίας ). Neuter (-ο, -ι) : Nouns ending in -ο change to -ου (το βιβλίο ➔ του βιβλίου ). Nouns ending in -ι change the final vowel to a diphthong -ιού (το παιδί ➔ του παιδιού ). Note: Only neuter nouns ending in -μα actually add a syllable (το πρόβλημα ➔ του προβλήματος ). 3. Possession: The "Of" Rule In English, we say "The teacher's book." In Greek, you must say "The book of the teacher." The object being owned comes first! Ο σκύλος του Γιώργου (George's dog / The dog of George). Το σπίτι της Μαρίας (Maria's house / The house of Maria). Το χρώμα του αυτοκινήτου (The car's color / The color of the car). 4. Indirect Objects (To Me / For Me) As you learned in Unit 13, the Genitive also marks the Receiver of an action. Μου δίνεις... (Give to me ...) Του λέω... (I am telling him ...) 🎯 Unit 15 Mastery Checklist I can use the correct Genitive articles: του, της, του (singular) and των (plural). I know that Greek structures possession as "The Item of the Owner." I can identify the Genitive case as both an "Owner" and a "Receiver." Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Family Tree & Possessions Up Next: Unit 16: Imperative Mood (Giving Orders)

idioms.title: Unit 28: Idioms & Remnants
idioms.content: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants To reach true fluency (B2/C1), you must understand the "ghosts" of the past. Modern Greek is filled with ancient expressions and colorful idioms that don't follow modern rules. 1. Ancient Dative Expressions Ancient Greek had a "Dative" case (for/to) that vanished in modern times, but these frozen phrases survived. You must learn them as whole blocks: Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime). Δόξα τω Θεώ (Thank God / Glory to God). Εν πάση περιπτώσει (In any case / Anyway). Τοις μετρητοίς (In cash). 2. Advanced Prepositions (Genitive) While basic prepositions take the Accusative, formal/ancient ones used in news and business take the Genitive . Λόγω + Genitive (Due to): Λόγω βροχής (Due to rain). Μέσω + Genitive (Via): Μέσω Αθηνών (Via Athens). Ενώπιον + Genitive (Before / In front of): Ενώπιον του νόμου (Before the law). 3. Colorful Idioms These are phrases where the literal meaning is completely different from the actual meaning. Έγινε της κακομοίρας! (A disaster happened! / Hell broke loose!) Χάνω τα λόγια μου. (I am lost for words). Πού το πας; (What are you getting at? / Where are you going with this?) Περαστικά! (Get well soon! / May it pass!) 4. Compound "Lego" Words Greek builds new words by snapping smaller ones together with the letter "ο" as glue. βιβλίο (book) + πωλείο (shop) ➔ βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore). ψάρι (fish) + ταβέρνα ➔ ψαροταβέρνα (fish tavern). 🎯 B2 Advanced Capstone Congratulations! You have navigated the complex waters of intermediate and advanced Greek grammar. You have mastered: Advanced Particles and Spoken Nuance. Relative and Reflexive Pronouns. Comparison of Adjectives. Participles and Impersonal Verbs. Formal Ancient Remnants. Up Next: Level C: Proficiency (Dialogue & Literature)

imperative.title: Unit 16: Imperative Mood
imperative.content: Unit 16: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders) The Imperative Mood is used to give commands, orders, or strong advice. In Greek, just like in the Future and Past, giving a command is deeply tied to Aspect (Video vs. Photo). 1. Simple Commands (The Photo: Do it once!) This is the most common way to give a command. You use the Simple Stem (Aorist) that you learned in Unit 12. Person Ending Example: γράφω ➔ γράψ- Εσύ (Informal) -ε Γράψε! (Write!) Εσείς (Formal/Plural) -τε Γράψτε! (Write!) Examples: Κλείσε την πόρτα! (Close the door!) Διαβάστε το βιβλίο! (Read the book!) 2. Continuous Commands (The Video: Keep doing it!) Used for habits or ongoing actions. You use the Continuous Stem (Present). Τρέχε! (Keep running!) Διάβαζε! (Keep reading [as a habit]!) TIP Formal/Plural Continuous : This form is identical to the normal Present tense! - Τρέχετε! (Keep running, everyone!) 3. The 5 Absolute Irregulars Like most languages, the most common commands in Greek are irregular. You MUST memorize these: Λέω (Say) ➔ Πες! / Πείτε! Βλέπω (Look) ➔ Δες! / Δείτε! Βρίσκω (Find) ➔ Βρες! / Βρείτε! Πίνω (Drink) ➔ Πιες! / Πιείτε! Έρχομαι (Come) ➔ Έλα! / Ελάτε! 4. Negative Commands: Μην To say "Don't," you use the magic particle μην (instead of δεν ) followed by the Subjunctive form. Μην κλείσεις την πόρτα. (Don't close the door—this time). Μην τρέχεις. (Don't run—habitually). 🎯 Unit 16 Mastery Checklist I can give a simple command using the Simple Stem ( Γράψε, Διάβασε ). I have memorized the "Big 5" irregular commands, especially Έλα! I know that negative commands use Μην + the Subjunctive. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Up Next: Unit 17: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner

impersonal-verbs.title: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs
impersonal-verbs.content: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions Sometimes a sentence doesn't have a specific "person" doing the action (e.g., "It is raining" or "It is necessary"). In Greek, we use Impersonal Verbs which are frozen in the 3rd Person Singular . 1. The Power Pair: Πρέπει & Μπορεί You’ve used these since A1, but now you understand why they work this way. They almost always trigger the Subjunctive ( να ). Πρέπει να... (It is necessary that... / Must ) Μπορεί να... (It is possible that... / Might ) WARNING Μπορώ vs Μπορεί - Μπορώ να πάω. (I can go—I have the ability). - Μπορεί να πάω. (I might go—it is a possibility). 2. Essential Intermediate Verbs These verbs describe allowed actions, appearances, or values. Greek Verb Meaning Example Απαγορεύεται It is forbidden Απαγορεύεται το κάπνισμα. Επιτρέπεται It is allowed Επιτρέπεται να μπεις. Φαίνεται It seems Φαίνεται ότι θα βρέξει. Αξίζει It is worth Αξίζει να το δεις. 3. Impersonal "Είναι" Expressions You can turn any adjective into a command or observation by using Είναι (It is) + να . Είναι δύσκολο να... (It is difficult to...) Είναι κρίμα να... (It is a pity to...) Είναι λογικό να... (It makes sense to...) 🎯 Unit 27 Mastery Checklist I know that impersonal verbs are frozen in the -ει or -εται forms. I understand the difference between Μπορώ (ability) and Μπορεί (possibility). I can use Απαγορεύεται and Επιτρέπεται correctly. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Expressions, impersonal states & weather Up Next: Unit 28: Idioms & Ancient Remnants

interrogative-pronouns.title: Unit 7: Asking Questions
interrogative-pronouns.content: Unit 7: Asking Questions (Τι, Πού, Ποιος) You now have a solid vocabulary and know how to conjugate verbs. The final step in your A1 foundation is learning how to ask for things. In Greek, this involves two parts: a special punctuation mark and "Question Words." 1. The Greek Question Mark (;) The first thing you must memorize is that the English semicolon ( ; ) is the Greek question mark. The English question mark ( ? ) does not exist in Greek. Πώς είσαι; (How are you?) Τι κάνεις; (What are you doing?) 2. The Fixed "W" Words These words are easy because they never change their shape. Τι (ti) = What? (e.g., Τι είναι αυτό;) Πού (poo) = Where? (e.g., Πού είσαι;) Πότε (pó-te) = When? (e.g., Πότε φεύγουμε;) Πώς (pos) = How? (e.g., Πώς είσαι;) Γιατί (ya-tí) = Why? (e.g., Γιατί λες όχι;) TIP Accent Mystery: You learned in Unit 1 that 1-syllable words don't get accents. Πού and Πώς are the rare exceptions! They keep the accent only to differentiate them from their twins που (that/who) and πως (that). 3. The Chameleon "W" Words Just like adjectives, some question words must match the gender of the noun you are asking about. "Who" or "Which" (Ποιος) Pronounced as one syllable: pyos ** Ποιος είναι ο άντρας; (Who is the man?) Ποια είναι η γυναίκα; (Who is the woman?) Ποιο παιδί; (Which child?) NOTE Accusative Alert! If "Who" is the object receiving the action, it drops its -ς just like the nouns you learned in Unit 2: Ποιον βλέπεις; ( Who(m) do you see? ) "How Much" or "How Many" (Πόσος) Πόσο κάνει; (How much does it cost?) Πόσα παιδιά έχεις; (How many children do you have? Matching the neuter plural -α ) 4. Word Order in Questions Greek is very flexible. To make a statement into a question, you often just change the punctuation and your tone of voice. Statement : Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ. (George is here.) Question : Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ; (Is George here?) 🎯 A1 Foundations Capstone Congratulations! You have officially completed the A1 Stage . You have mastered: The Writing System (Alphabet, Digraphs, Accents). Noun Basics (Genders, Articles, Accusative Case). Fundamental Verbs (Being, Having, Type A Verbs). Building basic negative sentences and questions. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Question Words & Pronouns Up Next: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives

nouns.title: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns
nouns.content: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns & Articles Now that you can read Greek, it's time to build sentences. In Greek, nouns (people, places, things) are "alive"—they change their shape based on Gender , Case , and Number . The most important rule for any Greek student is this: IMPORTANT The Golden Rule Never learn a Greek noun by itself. Always memorize it with its article ( ο, η, το ). The article tells you the gender and how the rest of the sentence must behave. 1. Grammatical Gender Every noun in Greek is either Masculine, Feminine, or Neuter. Usually, the last letter of the word tells you the gender. Gender Article ("The") Ending Example Translation Masculine ο -ος, -ας, -ης ο φίλ ος (o fílos) the (male) friend Feminine η -α, -η η αγάπ η (ee agapi) the love Neuter το -ο, -ι, -μα το σπίτ ι (to spíti) the house TIP Heads up! While 95% of nouns ending in -ος are masculine, some very common ones are actually feminine (like η οδός - the street or η μέθοδος - the method ). Always check the article! 2. Using "A" or "An" (Indefinite Articles) When talking about a non-specific thing (a car, a house), use the indefinite article. Notice how the endings often match the noun! Masculine : ένας (ένας φίλος) - a friend Feminine : μία / μια (μια αγάπη) - a love Neuter : ένα (ένα σπίτι) - a house 3. Intro to Cases: Subject vs. Object In English, "The man" is always "The man," whether he is doing the action or receiving it. In Greek, the word changes shape depending on its role in the sentence. The Nominative (The Subject) The form you find in the dictionary. Used for the person/thing performing the action. Ο πατέρ ας είναι εδώ. (The father is here) The Accusative (The Object) Used for the person/thing receiving the action (the Direct Object). Rule for Masculine: Drop the final -ς . The article becomes τον (always keep the 'ν' for masculine nouns to avoid confusion with the neuter article το !). Rule for Fem/Neu: The word doesn't change, but the feminine article becomes τη(ν) . Note: The feminine 'την' sometimes drops its 'ν' depending on the next letter, but you will learn that trick later! Case Masculine Feminine Neuter Subject (Nom) ο πατέρ ας η μητέρ α το παιδ ί Object (Acc) τον πατέρ α τη(ν) μητέρ α το παιδ ί TIP Names take articles! You don't say "Maria is here." You say "Η Μαρία είναι εδώ." (The Maria is here). 🎯 Unit 2 Mastery Checklist I can identify the gender of a noun by its ending. I know the direct articles ( ο, η, το ) by heart. I understand that masculine nouns drop the -ς when they are the object of a sentence. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Family & People Up Next: Unit 3: Being & Having

object-pronouns.title: Unit 13: Object Pronouns
object-pronouns.content: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns (me, to me) In Unit 3, you learned how to say "I see him " ( Τον βλέπω). These are Direct Objects . Now, we learn how to handle the receiver of an action: the Indirect Object (e.g., "Give to me "). 1. The Two Types of Objects Direct (Accusative) The target of the action. Με βλέπεις; (Do you see me ?) Τον ξέρω. (I know him .) Indirect (Genitive) The person receiving something. In Greek, we use the Genitive case for this! Μου δίνεις... (Give to me ...) Σου λέω... (I am telling to you ...) English Direct (Acc) Indirect (Gen) Me / To me με μου You / To you σε σου Him / To him τον του Her / To her τη(ν) της It / To it το του 2. Stacking Pronouns: The Rule What if you want to say "I give it (the book) to her (Maria)"? In Greek, the person (Indirect) always comes BEFORE the thing (Direct). Formula: Indirect + Direct + Verb Της το δίνω. (I give it to her). Μου το λες; (Are you telling it to me?) Σας τα γράφω. (I am writing them [neut] to you all). 3. Position in the Sentence Just like negation ( δεν ) and future ( θα ), these pronouns sit right in front of the verb. Δεν σου το δίνω. (I am not giving it to you). Θα μου το πεις; (Will you tell it to me?) 🎯 Unit 13 Mastery Checklist I know the difference between με (me) and μου (to me). I remember that the person comes before the thing: Της το δίνω. I can negate a double-pronoun sentence: Δεν σου το λέω. Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Up Next: Unit 14: The Past Tenses (Imperfect vs. Aorist)

participles.title: Unit 26: Participles
participles.content: Unit 26: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές) A Participle is a verb dressed up to act like an adjective or an adverb. In English, we use -ing (Running) or -ed (Locked). Greek has two powerful forms you must master at the B2 level. 1. Active Participle (-οντας) Used to show two actions happening at the same time by the same person. It translates to " while doing X " or " by doing X ." Formula: Stem + -οντας (or -ώντας for Type B) τρέχω ➔ τρέχοντας (while running) μιλάω ➔ μιλώντας (while speaking) TIP The Frozen Ending The active participle is indeclinable. It never changes to match gender or number. - Η Μαρία ήρθε τρέχοντας . (Maria came running). 2. Passive Participle (-μένος) This acts exactly like a normal Adjective. It describes the final state of something after an action (e.g., Tired, Broken, Hidden). Formula: Stem + -μένος, -μένη, -μένο κουράζω (tire) ➔ κουρασμένος (tired) κλείνω (close) ➔ κλεισμένος (closed) Because it is an adjective, it must match the noun: Η πόρτα είναι κλεισμένη. (The door is closed). Οι κλεισμένοι δρόμοι. (The closed roads). 3. The "C1" Bonus: -ώντας For Type B verbs (like αγαπάω or οδηγώ ), the ending becomes -ώντας (with an omega). Οδηγώντας (while driving) Γελώντας (while laughing) 🎯 Unit 26 Mastery Checklist I can use -οντας to show simultaneous actions. I know that the -οντας form never changes its ending. I can use -μένος as an adjective to describe a completed state. I know that -μένος must match the noun in gender and number. Up Next: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions

particles.title: Unit 22: Spoken Expressions
particles.content: Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Expressions To sound like a native, you need to master the tiny, untranslatable words that Greeks sprinkle into their speech. These "conversational particles" add flavor, doubt, sarcasm, or intense emotion to any sentence. 1. Curiosity & Doubt Άραγε (I wonder...) Placed at the beginning or end of a question. Άραγε θα έρθει η Μαρία; (I wonder if Maria will come?) Τι σκέφτεται άραγε ; (What is he thinking, I wonder?) Τάχα & Δήθεν (Supposedly / "Yeah, right") Used to show extreme sarcasm or disbelief. Κλαίει δήθεν επειδή χτύπησε. (He is supposedly crying [but I know he's faking]). Ναι, τάχα μου! (Yeah, right! / Allegedly!) 2. Wishes & Hopes Μακάρι (If only / Let's hope) Triggers the Subjunctive ( να ) or stands alone as a hopeful response. Μακάρι να περάσω τις εξετάσεις! (I hope I pass!) Person A: Θα βρούμε τραπέζι; ➔ Person B: Μακάρι! (Let's hope!) 3. Direction & Commands Ας (Let's / Let it be) Triggers the Subjunctive directly (no να needed). Ας πάμε! (Let's go!) Ας είναι. (Let it be / Whatever). Άντε / Άιντε (Come on!) The most versatile word in Greek. It can mean "Let's go!", "Hurry up!", or "Get out of here!" Άντε , σήκω! (Come on, get up!) Άντε πάλι... (Oh, here we go again...) 4. Emotional Interjections Μπράβο! (Well done!) Α μπα; (Really? / No way!) Αχ! (Oh! / Ah! - used for both pain and relief). Αλίμονο! (Alas! / Of course! - depending on context). 🎯 Unit 22 Mastery Checklist I can use Άραγε to express curiosity in a question. I know that Μακάρι expresses a strong wish. I understand the sarcastic tone of Δήθεν . I know how to say "Let's go" using Ας πάμε . Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Conversational Fillers & Particles Up Next: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (που, ο οποίος)

perfect-tenses.title: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses
perfect-tenses.content: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses (I have done) In Greek, the Perfect tenses (Present Perfect, Past Perfect, Future Perfect) are built almost exactly like in English. You use a helper verb " to have " combined with a special, unchanging action word. 1. The Helper Verb: Έχω You already know how to conjugate έχω . In this tense, it acts as the "motor" that drives the action. Εγώ έχω (I have) Εσύ έχεις (You have) Αυτός έχει (He has) Εμείς έχουμε (We have) Εσείς έχετε (You have) Αυτοί έχουν (They have) 2. The Frozen Word (-ει) To create the action word (e.g., "played", "read", "written"), you take the Simple Stem (from Unit 12) and add the ending -ει . This word never changes regardless of the person. παίζω ➔ παίξ- ➔ παίξει γράφω ➔ γράψ- ➔ γράψει διαβάζω ➔ διαβάσ- ➔ διαβάσει 3. Assembling the Present Perfect Simply put them together: Έχω παίξει. (I have played). Έχουν διαβάσει. (They have read). Έχεις γράψει; (Have you written?) 4. Past & Future Perfect To change the timeline, you only change the helper verb έχω . The frozen word stays the same! Past Perfect (I had done) Use the past tense helper είχα : Είχα παίξει. (I had played). Future Perfect (I will have done) Use the future helper θα έχω : Θα έχω παίξει μέχρι αύριο. (I will have played by tomorrow). 🎯 Unit 20 Mastery Checklist I can form the frozen "-ει" word using the simple stem. I know that the helper verb έχω is the only part that conjugates. I can translate "I have", "I had", and "I will have" using this formula. Practice Vocabulary: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences Up Next: Unit 21: Conditionals (The 'If' Clauses)

plurals.title: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives
plurals.content: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives Welcome to the A2 Expansion Stage ! You’ve mastered the singular. Now, it’s time to multiply everything. In Greek, when a noun becomes plural, its Article , its Ending , and its Adjective must all change to match. 1. Masculine Plurals (Οι / Τους) Masculine nouns are the most "active" during pluralization. The ending depends on the singular form. Singular Plural Subject (Nom) Plural Object (Acc) Pattern ο φίλος οι φίλ οι τους φίλ ους -ος ➔ -οι / -ους ο πατέρας οι πατέρ ες τους πατέρ ες -ας ➔ -ες ο μαθητής οι μαθητ ές τους μαθητ ές -ης ➔ -ες WARNING The -οι vs -ους Trap For nouns ending in -ος , the plural "The" is οι (Subject) but becomes τους (Object). The noun ending follows: -οι matches οι , and -ους matches τους . - Οι καλοί φίλοι. (The good friends - Subject) - Τους καλούς φίλους. (The good friends - Object) 2. Feminine Plurals (Οι / Τις) Feminine nouns are much easier. The noun ending -ες never changes between subject and object—only the article flips! Singular : Η μητέρα / Η καλή Plural Subject : Οι μητέρ ες / Οι καλ ές Plural Object : Τις μητέρ ες / Τις καλ ές CAUTION The Academic Exception (-εις) While everyday words use -ες , many formal or academic nouns ending in -ση or -ξη form their plural with -εις . - Η λύση (solution) ➔ Οι λύσεις - Η τάξη (class) ➔ Οι τάξεις WARNING The Masked Feminines (-ος) Remember η οδός (the street) from Unit 2? Feminine nouns ending in -ος take masculine endings in the plural ( -οι, -ους ), but keep their feminine articles! - Plural Subject: Οι οδ οί - Plural Object: Τις οδ ούς 3. Neuter Plurals (Τα) Neuter nouns are the most stable. Both the article τα and the noun endings remain the same whether they are the subject or the object. Singular Plural (Nom & Acc) Pattern το βιβλίο τα βιβλί α -ο ➔ -α το παιδί τα παιδ ιά -ι ➔ -ια το μάθημα τα μαθή ματα -μα ➔ -ματα 4. Verb Agreement When the subject is plural, the verb must also be plural. Ο φίλος διαβάζει . (The friend reads.) Οι φίλοι διαβάζουν . (The friends read.) 🎯 Unit 8 Mastery Checklist I can pluralize the articles: οι/τους , οι/τις , and τα . I know that masculine -ος nouns change to -οι (subject) and -ους (object). I can correctly pluralize neuter nouns ending in -μα (e.g., προβλήματα ). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Up Next: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2

reflexive-pronouns.title: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns
reflexive-pronouns.content: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself) Reflexive pronouns are used when the person doing the action and the person receiving it are the same. In English, we use "-self." In Greek, we use the phrase ο εαυτός μου (my self). 1. The Formula The word εαυτός (self) is a masculine noun. To say "myself," you simply add the weak possessive pronoun after it. Person Greek English 1st (Singular) τον εαυτό μου myself 2nd (Singular) τον εαυτό σου yourself 3rd (Singular) τον εαυτό του / της himself / herself 1st (Plural) τους εαυτούς μας ourselves 2nd (Plural) τους εαυτούς σας yourselves 3rd (Plural) τους εαυτούς τους themselves 2. How to Use them Because "the self" is receiving the action, it is almost always in the Accusative Case . Αγαπώ τον εαυτό μου. (I love myself). Προσέχεις τον εαυτό σου; (Do you take care of yourself?) Σκέφτονται μόνο τους εαυτούς τους. (They only think of themselves). 3. Reflexive Verbs vs. Pronouns In Unit 18, you learned that Passive Verbs (-ομαι) are often used reflexively. Πλένομαι (I wash myself). So when do you use the pronoun? You use the pronoun ( τον εαυτό μου ) for emphasis or with verbs that don't have a passive form. Βλάπτω τον εαυτό μου. (I am hurting myself). 🎯 Unit 24 Mastery Checklist I can form the reflexive phrase for any person (e.g., τον εαυτό της ). I know that εαυτός is a masculine noun and uses masculine articles. I understand when to use the pronoun instead of a passive verb ending. Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Up Next: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison (Better, Faster)

relative-pronouns.title: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns
relative-pronouns.content: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That) Relative pronouns link two sentences together. Instead of saying "I saw a man. The man was tall," you say "I saw a man who was tall." In Greek, we have two ways to do this: the easy way and the formal way. 1. The Easy Way: Που The word που is the ultimate chameleon. It is indeclinable (never changes shape) and can mean who , which , that , or where . Ο άντρας που είδα... (The man [whom] I saw...) Το σπίτι που μένω... (The house [where] I live...) Οι άνθρωποι που ξέρω... (The people [that] I know...) TIP Spelling Alert : Don't confuse που (that/which) with Πού; (Where?). The question word always has an accent; the relative pronoun never does. 2. The Formal Way: Ο οποίος If you are writing a formal letter or reading the news, you will see ο οποίος . Unlike που , this word acts like an adjective and must match the noun in Gender, Number, and Case . Ο άντρας ο οποίος... (Masc) Η γυναίκα η οποία... (Fem) Το παιδί το οποίο... (Neut) 3. Using Prepositions When you need to say things like "the man to whom " or "the house in which ," you must use the formal ο οποίος or a combination of που . Το σπίτι στο οποίο μένω. (The house in which I live). Η κοπέλα για την οποία σου μίλησα. (The girl about whom I told you). 4. The Relative "What": Ό,τι When you want to say "whatever" or "what" (meaning "that which"), use ό,τι . Κάνε ό,τι θέλεις. (Do whatever you want). Ό,τι πεις. (Whatever you say). 🎯 Unit 23 Mastery Checklist I can use που to link two basic sentences. I know the difference between που (that) and πού; (where?). I recognize ο οποίος as the formal version of "who/which." I can use ό,τι to mean "whatever." Practice Vocabulary: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Up Next: Unit 24: Reflexive Pronouns (Myself)

simple-stem.title: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem
simple-stem.content: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem (Σ, Ξ, Ψ) To speak in the Future or the Subjunctive, you need the Simple Stem (the "Photo" stem). Finding this stem is like a puzzle: you look at the ending of the verb and swap it for a new one. There are three main "Cheat Codes" to master. 1. The "S" Group (Σ) ➔ Most common If the verb ends in -νω, -ζω, -ω, or -θω , the stem usually changes to a -σ- . διαβάζω (read) ➔ διαβάσ- κλείνω (close) ➔ κλείσ- πληρώνω (pay) ➔ πληρώσ- νιώθω (feel) ➔ νιώσ- 2. The "X" Group (Ξ) ➔ Throat sounds If the verb ends in a "throat" sound like -κω, -γω, -χω , or a specific -ζω , it explodes into a -ξ- (Xi). ανοίγω (open) ➔ ανοίξ- τρέχω (run) ➔ τρέξ- διδάσκω (teach) ➔ διδάξ- παίζω (play) ➔ παίξ- (The main exception!) 3. The "PS" Group (Ψ) ➔ Lip sounds If the verb ends in a "lip" sound like -πω, -βω, -φω , it explodes into a -ψ- (Psi). γράφω (write) ➔ γράψ- λείπω (be absent) ➔ λείψ- κρύβω (hide) ➔ κρύψ- 4. Why does this matter? Now that you have the stem (e.g., γράψ- ), you can use it for everything you learned in Unit 10 and 11: Future : Θα γράψ ω. (I will write). Subjunctive : Θέλω να γράψ ω. (I want to write). IMPORTANT Irregulars Some verbs are rebels and don't follow these codes. You just have to memorize their simple stems: - βλέπω (see) ➔ δ- (θα δω) - λέω (say) ➔ π- (θα πω) - πάω (go) ➔ πα- (θα πάω) - έρχομαι (come) ➔ ερθ- (θα έρθω) 🎯 Unit 12 Mastery Checklist I can turn διαβάζω into διαβάσ- . I can turn γράφω into γράψ- . I can turn ανοίγω into ανοίξ- . I have memorized the "Big 4" irregular stems ( δω, πω, πάω, έρθω ). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Food & the Restaurant Up Next: Unit 13: Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns

subjunctive-1.title: Unit 10: Aspect & Subjunctive
subjunctive-1.content: Unit 10: Verbal Aspect & The Subjunctive (Να) Welcome to the great divide in the Greek language. To move from A1 to A2, you must understand Aspect . Greek doesn’t just care about when something happens; it cares about how it happens. 1. The "Video vs. Photo" Concept Greek forces you to choose between two ways of looking at an action: Continuous Aspect (The Video) : For habits, routines, or ongoing background actions. (e.g., "I am playing", "I play every day"). Simple Aspect (The Photo) : For a single, specific, or completed event. (e.g., "I want to play one game "). Because of this, every active Greek verb has two stems : Continuous Stem : The one you know (παίζ-). Simple Stem : The "Aorist" stem (παίξ-). We will learn how to build this in the next units. 2. The Subjunctive Mood (Magic word: Να) Greek has no "infinitive" (like "to go"). Instead, it uses να + a conjugated verb. English Greek (Literal) Greek (Actual) I want to go . I want that I go . Θέλω να πάω. You want to play . You want that you play . Θέλεις να παίζεις; When to use Να: θα + να : "Θέλω να", "Μπορώ να", "Πρέπει να" (I must). Commands : Να προσέχεις! (Be careful!). Wishes : Να είσαι καλά. (Be well). 3. Aspect in the Subjunctive Since να is followed by a verb, you must choose the aspect: Θέλω να παίζω (Continuous): I want to be playing (regularly, as a hobby). Θέλω να παίξω (Simple): I want to play (this specific game right now). TIP The Secret of "Simple" Most of the time, when you ask for something or want to do something, you are talking about a specific event. Therefore, the Simple Stem is the most common one used with να . 🎯 Unit 10 Mastery Checklist I understand the difference between a "Video" (Continuous) and a "Photo" (Simple) action. I can use θα + να to translate English "to" phrases. I know that Greeks conjugate both verbs (e.g., Θέλω να πάω vs Θέλεις να πας ). Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Up Next: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα)

verb-groups-b.title: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2
verb-groups-b.content: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2 (The Accented Verbs) In Unit 4, you learned Type A verbs (like κάνω ), where the accent is on the stem. Now we meet Type B —verbs where the accent is on the very last letter ( -ώ ). These verbs are the "singers" of Greek; they have a distinct rhythm and are split into two "vowel teams". Team B1: The "Alpha" Lovers (-άω) These are the most common Type B verbs. They love the letter α . Examples: αγαπώ (αγαπάω), μιλάω, ρωτάω, ξυπνάω **. Pronoun Ending Example: μιλάω (speak) (Εγώ) -άω μιλ άω (Εσύ) -άς μιλ άς (Αυτός/ή/ό) -άει μιλ άει (Εμείς) -άμε μιλ άμε (Εσείς) -άτε μιλ άτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -άνε μιλ άνε TIP Spoken vs. Written You might see αγαπώ (Formal) and αγαπάω (Spoken). They are the same! For a natural Masterclass feel, we recommend the -άω pattern as it’s the most common in modern Greece. Team B2: The "Oo" Lovers (-ώ) These verbs are less common but very important. They prefer the -ούμε sound in the plural. Examples: μπορώ, ζω, τηλεφωνώ, οδηγώ **. Pronoun Ending Example: μπορώ (can) (Εγώ) -ώ μπορ ώ (Εσύ) -είς μπορ είς (Αυτός/ή/ό) -εί μπορ εί (Εμείς) -ούμε μπορ ούμε (Εσείς) -είτε μπορ είτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ούν μπορ ούν How to tell them apart? If a verb has an -άω version (like μιλάω ), it's B1 . If it ONLY ends in -ώ (like μπορώ ), it's B2 . 🎯 Unit 9 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate μιλάω (B1) and μπορώ (B2) in the present tense. I know that B1 verbs use -άμε and -άτε for 'we' and 'you all'. I recognize that B2 endings look like Type A endings but with an extra vowel "kick." Practice Vocabulary: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Up Next: Unit 10: The Subjunctive Mood (Intro to 'να')

verb-to-be.title: Unit 3: Being & Having
verb-to-be.content: Unit 3: Being & Having (Είμαι & Έχω) To build real sentences, you need verbs. In Greek, just like in English, the two most important verbs are To Be (I am) and To Have (I have). Together, they unlock hundreds of basic conversations. 1. The Actors: Personal Pronouns Before the verbs, we need to know the people. Greek handles "I, You, He" very logically. English Greek Pronunciation I Εγώ (e-gó) You (sing.) Εσύ (e-sí) He / She / It Αυτός / Αυτή / Αυτό (af-tós / af-tí / af-tó) We Εμείς (e-mís) You (plur./formal) Εσείς (e-sís) They (m/f/n) Αυτοί / Αυτές / Αυτά (af-tí / af-tés / af-tá) NOTE Rhyme Time Notice that Εμείς (We) and Εσείς (You plural) rhyme, and the 3rd person singular pronouns ( Αυτός, Αυτή, Αυτό ) follow the exact same ending rules as the nouns you learned in Unit 2! TIP Pronunciation Tip: When Alpha and Upsilon ( Αυ ) team up before certain letters like Tau (τ), they make an 'af' sound (like in af-tós )! 2. Conjugating the "Big Two" Είμαι (To Be) Used for identity and state (I am a student, I am here). Είμαι (I am) Είσαι (You are) Είναι (He/She/It is) Είμαστε (We are) Είστε (You are) Είναι (They are — Surprise! It's the same word as "He is"! ) Έχω (To Have) Used for possession and family. Έχω (I have) Έχεις (You have) Έχει (He/She/It has) Έχουμε (We have) Έχετε (You have) Έχουν (ε) (They have) 3. The Pro-Drop Rule: Speak Like a Native In English, you must say " I have a dog." In Greek, you simply say " Έχω έναν σκύλο." NOTE Notice it's "έναν" and not "ένας"? Just like the definite articles you learned in Unit 2, the word for "a/an" also changes its ending when it's the Object of the sentence receiving the action! Because the ending of the verb changes for every person (I, you, he), the pronoun Εγώ, Εσύ, κτλ. becomes redundant. Greeks only use the pronouns when they want to emphasize who specifically they are talking about. Natural : Είμαι εδώ. (I am here.) Emphatic : Εγώ είμαι εδώ! ( I am here!) 4. The Power Combo With these two verbs and the Accusative case from Unit 2, you can already speak! Έχεις το βιβλίο; (Do you have the book?) Όχι, ο Γιώργος έχει το βιβλίο. (No, George has the book.) Είμαστε φίλοι. (We are friends.) 🎯 Unit 3 Mastery Checklist I can conjugate είμαι and έχω by heart. I know when to use Αυτός, Αυτή, and Αυτό . I understand that I should drop the pronoun when speaking naturally. Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Everyday Objects & Places Up Next: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Group A)

verbs.title: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Type A)
verbs.content: Unit 4: Regular Verbs & Negation (Type A) You’ve learned the nouns; now it’s time to make things happen. Verbs are the workhorses of the Greek sentence. They change their shape to tell you who is doing the action and when . 1. The Anatomy of a Verb Every Greek verb has a Stem (trunk) and an Ending (leaves). Take the verb "to play": παίζω (pé-zo) . The Stem is παίζ- (contains the meaning). The Ending is -ω (means "I"). Almost all Greek verbs in the dictionary end in -ω . This always means "I" am doing the action. 2. Verb Groups (The Three Teams) Before conjugating, you must know which "team" a verb belongs to. Look at the accent mark in the dictionary form: Type A (Regular) : Accent is on the stem (NOT the -ω). Examples: παίζω, κάνω, γράφω, διαβάζω ** Type B (Accented) : Accent is on the ending ( -ώ or -άω ). Examples: αγαπώ (αγαπάω), μιλώ (μιλάω) ** Type C (Passive) : Ends in -ομαι instead of -ω. Examples: έρχομαι, γίνομαι ** In this unit, we focus exclusively on Type A. 3. Conjugation (Present Tense) To change the person (I, you, we...), chop off the -ω and add these endings: Pronoun Ending Example: διαβάζω (read) (Εγώ) -ω διαβάζ ω (Εσύ) -εις διαβάζ εις (Αυτός/ή/ό) -ει διαβάζ ει (Εμείς) -ουμε διαβάζ ουμε (Εσείς) -ετε διαβάζ ετε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ουν(ε) διαβάζ ουν TIP He = They? In English, "He is" and "They are" are different. In Greek, for verbs like Είμαι , they are the same ( είναι ). But for regular verbs like διαβάζω , They ( -ουν ) is distinct from He ( -ει ). To say "I do not," just put the word δεν ( den ) right before the verb. Δεν παίζω ( I don't play ) Δεν κάνω ( I don't do/make ) IMPORTANT Δεν vs. Όχι - Use Όχι to answer "No" to a question. - Use Δεν right before a verb to say "not" ( Δεν καταλαβαίνω — I don't understand ). - Example: "Όχι, δεν παίζω" ( No, I don't play ). 🎯 Unit 4 Mastery Checklist I can tell a Type A verb apart from a Type B by its accent. I have memorized the endings: -ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν . I know that δεν is used for verbs, while όχι is used for "No." Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Essential Action Verbs Up Next: Unit 5: The Accusative Case

vocabulary-action-verbs.title: Essential Action Verbs
vocabulary-action-verbs.content: A1 Vocabulary: Essential Action Verbs The most common Type A verbs to get you speaking right away. 1. The Big Two είμαι (í-me) = to be έχω (é-ho) = to have 2. Action & Interaction κάνω (ká-no) = to do / to make διαβάζω (dya-vá-zo) = to read γράφω (grá-fo) = to write παίζω (pé-zo) = to play βλέπω (vlé-po) = to see / to watch 3. Thought & Emotion καταλαβαίνω (ka-ta-la-vé-no) = to understand θέλω (thé-lo) = to want Practice Grammar: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Type A)

vocabulary-adjectives-comparisons.title: Adjectives & Comparisons
vocabulary-adjectives-comparisons.content: B2 Vocabulary: Adjectives & Comparisons Words for evaluating the world around you and comparing things. 1. The Essential Evaluators καλός, -ή, -ό (ka-lós) = good ➔ καλύτερος (better) κακός, -ή, -ό (ka-kós) = bad ➔ χειρότερος (worse) πολύς, πολλή, πολύ (po-lís) = much / many ➔ περισσότερος (more) λίγος, -η, -ο (lí-gos) = little / few ➔ λιγότερος (less) 2. Describing Size & Shape μεγάλος, -η, -ο (me-gá-los) = big ➔ μεγαλύτερος (bigger / older) μικρός, -ή, -ό (mik-rós) = small ➔ μικρότερος (smaller / younger) ψηλός, -ή, -ό (psee-lós) = tall κοντός, -ή, -ό (kon-dós) = short μακρύς, -ιά, -ύ (mak-rís) = long 3. Describing Quality νέος, -α, -ο / καινούριος (né-os) = new / young παλιός, -ά, -ό (pa-lyós) = old (for things) ωραίος, -α, -ο / όμορφος (o-ré-os) = beautiful / nice άσχημος, -η, -ο (ás-hee-mos) = ugly 4. Comparison Keywords πιο... (pyo) = more... (the easy way: πιο ωραίος ) από... (a-pó) = than... ( πιο ψηλός από εμένα ) ο πιο... (o pyo) = the most... (the superlative) Practice Grammar: Unit 25: Adjective Comparison

vocabulary-affection-emotion.title: Affection & Emotion
vocabulary-affection-emotion.content: C1 Vocabulary: Affection & Emotion Vocabulary specifically tailored to practice Diminutives and Augmentatives . Greeks use these words constantly to show love, familiarity, or emphasis. 1. Terms of Endearment (Υποκοριστικά) Add your new suffixes (-άκι, -ούλα) to these base words to show affection! η αγάπη (ee a-gá-pee) = the love ➔ η αγαπούλα (sweetheart) η ψυχή (ee psee-heé) = the soul ➔ η ψυχούλα (sweet soul) η καρδιά (ee kar-dyá) = the heart ➔ η καρδούλα (little heart) το μωρό (to mo-ró) = the baby ➔ το μωράκι (little baby) ο φίλος (o fí-los) = friend ➔ το φιλαράκι (buddy / pal) 2. Everyday Diminutives (Το -άκι) Almost any object can take the -άκι suffix to sound friendlier or smaller. το σπίτι (to spí-tee) = house ➔ το σπιτάκι (little house) ο καφές (o ka-fés) = coffee ➔ το καφεδάκι (a nice, relaxing coffee) το νερό (to ne-ró) = water ➔ το νεράκι (a little water) το φαγητό (to fa-yee-tó) = food ➔ το φαγητάκι (a nice meal) 3. Augmentatives (Μεγεθυντικά) When things are unusually large, impressive, or scary! ο σκύλος (o skí-los) = dog ➔ ο σκύλαρος (huge dog) το αυτοκίνητο (to af-to-kí-nee-to) = car ➔ ο αυτοκινητάρας (impressive car) ο άντρας (o án-dras) = man ➔ ο άντραρος (big man) η φωνή (ee fo-ní) = voice ➔ η φωνάκλα (loud, booming voice) Practice Grammar: Unit 29: Diminutives

vocabulary-ancient-phrases.title: Ancient Phrases in Modern Greek
vocabulary-ancient-phrases.content: C2 Vocabulary: Ancient Phrases in Modern Greek Modern Greek is heavily sprinkled with direct survivals from Ancient Greek. These phrases are used in formal settings, the news, and even everyday conversation to sound educated or poetic. 1. Expressions of Time & Status εν τω μεταξύ (en to me-tax-í) = in the meantime / meanwhile καταρχάς / καταρχήν (ka-tar-hás) = first of all / to begin with εκ των προτέρων (ek ton pro-té-ron) = in advance (e.g. "thank you in advance") εκ των υστέρων (ek ton ees-té-ron) = in hindsight / after the fact τουλάχιστον (too-lá-hees-ton) = at least (Note: this is an ancient superlative!) 2. Expressions of Agreement & Condition δόξα τω Θεώ (dóx-a to the-ó) = thank God (literally: glory to God) παρεμπιπτόντως (pa-rem-peep-tón-dos) = by the way / incidentally ως επί το πλείστον (os e-pí to plís-ton) = for the most part / mostly επί τούτου (e-pí toó-too) = on purpose / intentionally λόγου χάρη (ló-goo há-ree) = for example / "for the sake of the word" 3. Academic & Formal Nouns (The -σις / -ση group) These feminine nouns originally ended in -σις in ancient Greek. η λύση / λύσις (ee lí-see) = the solution η κρίση / κρίσις (ee krí-see) = the crisis / judgment η γνώση / γνώσις (ee gnó-see) = the knowledge η δήλωση / δήλωσις (ee dí-lo-see) = the statement / declaration

vocabulary-beliefs-society.title: Beliefs, Relations & Society
vocabulary-beliefs-society.content: B2 Vocabulary: Beliefs, Relations & Society Vocabulary designed to pair with Relative/Reflexive pronouns and advanced interpersonal discussions. 1. Reflexives & Relations ο εαυτός μου (o e-af-tós moo) = myself (used constantly in B2!) ο ένας τον άλλον (o é-nas ton ál-lon) = each other / one another η σχέση (ee shé-see) = the relationship το πρόβλημα (to pró-vlee-ma) = the problem ο κόσμος (o kós-mos) = the world / the "people" 2. Expressing Beliefs & Abstract Verbs πιστεύω (pis-tév-o) = to believe νομίζω (no-mí-zo) = to think (as an opinion: Νομίζω ότι... ) συμφωνώ (sim-fo-nó) = to agree διαφωνώ (dya-fo-nó) = to disagree ελπίζω (el-pí-zo) = to hope αλλάζω (a-lá-zo) = to change 3. Abstract Nouns for Conversation η ζωή (ee zo-í) = the life η αλήθεια (ee a-lí-thya) = the truth το ψέμα (to psé-ma) = the lie η ιδέα (ee ee-dé-a) = the idea (Plural: οι ιδέες ) η δουλειά (ee doo-lyá) = the work / job (Plural: οι δουλειές ) Practice Grammar: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns

vocabulary-colors-numbers.title: Colors & Numbers
vocabulary-colors-numbers.content: A1 Vocabulary: Colors & Numbers Describing quantities and colors. 1. Numbers 1-10 (Αριθμοί) ένα (é-na) = one δύο (dí-o) = two τρία (trí-a) = three τέσσερα (tés-se-ra) = four πέντε (pén-de) = five έξι (é-xi) = six επτά (ep-tá) = seven οκτώ (ok-tó) = eight εννέα (en-né-a) = nine δέκα (dé-ka) = ten 2. Basic Colors (Χρώματα) κόκκινος (kók-ki-nos) = red μαύρος (máv-ros) = black πράσινος (prá-si-nos) = green μπλε (ble) = blue ροζ (roz) = pink καφέ (ka-fé) = brown Practice Grammar: Unit 6: Adjectives & Agreement

vocabulary-compound-everyday.title: Common Compound Words
vocabulary-compound-everyday.content: C1 Vocabulary: Common Compound Words Greek loves to smash words together. Here are some of the most common compound words you will encounter every day, blending two concepts into a single word. 1. Time & Calendar Blends το Σαββατοκύριακο (to sa-va-to-kí-rya-ko) = the weekend (Saturday + Sunday) το μερόνυχτο (to me-ró-neeh-to) = a 24-hour period (day + night) το ημερολόγιο (to ee-me-ro-ló-yee-o) = the calendar / diary (day + word/logic) 2. Objects & Places το μαχαιροπίρουνο (to ma-he-ro-pí-roo-no) = cutlery (knife + fork) το αλατοπίπερο (to a-la-to-pí-pe-ro) = salt and pepper set το βιβλιοπωλείο (to vee-vli-o-po-lí-o) = the bookstore (book + to sell) το ανθοπωλείο (to an-tho-po-lí-o) = the florist (flower + to sell) το ζαχαροπλαστείο (to za-ha-ro-plas-tí-o) = the pastry shop (sugar + to mold) 3. Descriptive Compounds ο καλοκάγαθος (o ka-lo-ká-ga-thos) = good-hearted / kind (good + good) ο στενοκέφαλος (o ste-no-ké-fa-los) = narrow-minded / stubborn (narrow + head) ο χαμογελαστός (o ha-mo-ye-las-tós) = smiling (ground / low + laugh) η καλημέρα (ee ka-lee-mé-ra) = the good morning greeting (good + day) 4. Work & Society η πολυκατοικία (ee po-lee-ka-tee-kí-a) = apartment building (many + residence) η τηλεόραση (ee tee-le-ó-ra-see) = the television (far + vision) το λεωφορείο (to le-o-fo-rí-o) = the bus (people + carrier)

vocabulary-deponent-states.title: Deponent Verbs & States
vocabulary-deponent-states.content: B1 Vocabulary: Deponent Verbs & States Master the most common -ομαι verbs that describe thoughts, feelings, and body states. 1. Thoughts & Memories σκέφτομαι (ské-fto-me) = to think / to consider θυμάμαι (thee-má-me) = to remember ξεχνιέμαι (xe-hnyé-me) = to be forgotten / to get distracted αισθάνομαι (es-thá-no-me) = to feel 2. Daily Routines (Reflexive actions) κοιμάμαι (kee-má-me) = to sleep σηκώνομαι (see-kó-no-me) = to get up / stand up κάθομαι (ká-tho-me) = to sit πλένομαι (plé-no-me) = to wash oneself λούζομαι (loo-zo-me) = to wash one's hair 3. Interaction έρχομαι (ér-ho-me) = to come (Aorist: ήρθα ) γίνομαι (yí-no-me) = to become / to happen χρειάζομαι (hree-á-zo-me) = to need δανείζομαι (da-ní-zo-me) = to borrow Practice Grammar: Unit 18: Passive & Deponent Verbs

vocabulary-directions-commands.title: Directions & Commands
vocabulary-directions-commands.content: B1 Vocabulary: Directions & Commands Combine these words with the Imperative Mood and Adverbs to navigate the world. 1. Navigating the City (Αξιοθέατα) ο δρόμος (o dró-mos) = the road / street το ξενοδοχείο (to xe-no-do-hí-o) = the hotel το φαρμακείο (to far-ma-kí-o) = the pharmacy το νοσοκομείο (to no-so-ko-mí-o) = the hospital το πάρκο (to pár-ko) = the park το αεροδρόμιο (to a-e-ro-dró-mee-o) = the airport 2. Giving Directions (Imperatives) Πήγαινε / Πηγαίνετε! (pí-ye-ne) = Go! (Singular / Plural) Στρίψε / Στρίψτε! (stríp-se) = Turn! Προχώρα / Προχωρήστε! (pro-hó-ra) = Go ahead / Move forward! Σταμάτα / Σταματήστε! (sta-má-ta) = Stop! 3. Directional Adverbs δεξιά (dex-yá) = right αριστερά (a-ris-te-rá) = left ευθεία (ef-thí-a) = straight ahead απέναντι (a-pé-nan-di) = opposite / across δίπλα σε (díp-la) = next to Practice Grammar: Unit 16: Imperative Mood

vocabulary-everyday-objects.title: Everyday Objects & Places
vocabulary-everyday-objects.content: A1 Vocabulary: Everyday Objects & Places Words for the things and places around you. 1. Top Objects το βιβλίο (to vee-vlí-o) = book η τσάντα (ee tsán-da) = bag η πόρτα (ee pór-ta) = door το αυτοκίνητο (to af-to-kí-nee-to) = car 2. Food & Drink το νερό (to ne-ró) = water ο καφές (o ka-fés) = coffee η ζάχαρη (ee zá-ha-ree) = sugar 3. Places & Environment το σπίτι (to spí-tee) = house / home η οδός (ee o-dós) = street το σινεμά (to see-ne-má) = cinema ο ουρανός (o oo-ra-nós) = sky η θάλασσα (ee thá-las-sa) = sea Practice Grammar: Unit 5: The Accusative Case

vocabulary-family-people.title: Family & People
vocabulary-family-people.content: A1 Vocabulary: Family & People Learn the essential words to talk about people and family members in Greek. 1. Immediate Family (Οικογένεια) ο πατέρας (o pa-té-ras) = father η μητέρα (ee mee-té-ra) = mother ο μπαμπάς (o ba-bás) = dad το παιδί (to pe-dí) = child 2. People (Άνθρωποι) ο άντρας (o án-dras) = man / husband η γυναίκα (ee yee-né-ka) = woman / wife ο φίλος (o fí-los) = friend (male) η φίλη (ee fí-lee) = friend (female) 3. Professionals ο δάσκαλος / η δασκάλα (o dás-ka-los / ee das-ká-la) = teacher ο/η γιατρός (o/ee ya-trós) = doctor Practice Grammar: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns

vocabulary-family-possessions.title: Family Tree & Possessions
vocabulary-family-possessions.content: B1 Vocabulary: Family Tree & Possessions Perfect vocabulary for practicing the Genitive Case (e.g. "The brother of the mother"). 1. Extended Family ο αδερφός / ο αδελφός (o a-der-fós) = the brother η αδερφή / η αδελφή (ee a-der-fí) = the sister ο παππούς (o pa-poós) = the grandfather (Genitive: του παππού ) η γιαγιά (ee ya-yá) = the grandmother (Genitive: της γιαγιάς ) ο θείος (o thí-os) = the uncle η θεία (ee thí-a) = the aunt ο ξάδερφος / η ξαδέρφη (o xá-der-fos) = the cousin 2. Belongings & Pets το κλειδί (to klee-dí) = the key (Plural: τα κλειδιά , Genitive: του κλειδιού ) το κινητό (τηλέφωνο) (to kee-nee-tó) = the mobile phone ο υπολογιστής (o ee-po-lo-yee-stís) = the computer ο σκύλος (o skí-los) = the dog η γάτα (ee gá-ta) = the cat 3. Question Words for Genitive Ποιανού είναι...; (pya-noo eé-ne) = Whose is it? (Masculine / Neuter owner) Ποιανής είναι...; (pya-nís eé-ne) = Whose is it? (Feminine owner) Τίνος είναι...; (tí-nos eé-ne) = Whose is it? (Formal / Generic) Practice Grammar: Unit 15: Genitive Case

vocabulary-food-restaurant.title: Food & the Restaurant
vocabulary-food-restaurant.content: A2 Vocabulary: Food & the Restaurant Vocabulary meant to be combined with the Simple Past (I ate) and Subjunctive (I want to eat) alongside plural objects. 1. Restaurant Basics το εστιατόριο (to e-stya-tó-ryo) = the restaurant η ταβέρνα (ee ta-vér-na) = the tavern το μενού / ο κατάλογος (to me-noo / o ka-tá-lo-gos) = the menu / catalog το πιάτο (to pyá-to) = the plate (Plural: τα πιάτα) το ποτήρι (to po-tí-ree) = the glass (Plural: τα ποτήρια) 2. Eating and Drinking Verbs (Aorist Stem changes) τρώω (tró-o) = to eat (Aorist: έφαγα / Subjunctive: να φάω ) πίνω (pí-no) = to drink (Aorist: ήπια / Subjunctive: να πιω ) παραγγέλνω (pa-ran-gél-no) = to order 3. Essential Foods το ψωμί (to pso-mí) = the bread το κρέας (to kré-as) = the meat το ψάρι (to psá-ree) = the fish το τυρί (to tee-rí) = the cheese τα φρούτα (ta froo-ta) = the fruits (usually used in plural) τα λαχανικά (ta la-ha-nee-ká) = the vegetables 4. Useful Phrases Τι θα πάρετε; (ti tha pá-re-te?) = What will you have/take? Μπορώ να έχω...; (bo-ró na é-ho...) = Can I have...? Practice Grammar: Unit 12: Building the Simple Stem

vocabulary-impersonal-weather.title: Expressions, impersonal states & weather
vocabulary-impersonal-weather.content: B2 Vocabulary: Expressions & Impersonal States Verbs and nouns that are frozen in the 3rd person singular to describe states, rules, and the environment. 1. Impersonal Rule Verbs (The "It" Verbs) πρέπει (να) (pré-pee) = it is necessary (must) μπορεί (να) (bo-reé) = it is possible (might) απαγορεύεται (a-pa-go-rév-e-te) = it is forbidden επιτρέπεται (e-pee-trép-e-te) = it is allowed αξίζει (ax-í-zee) = it is worth it φαίνεται (fén-e-te) = it seems / it looks like 2. Weather & Nature (Τα Φαινόμενα) Weather is almost always impersonal in Greek. ο καιρός (o ke-rós) = the weather βρέχει (vré-hee) = it is raining / η βροχή = the rain χιονίζει (hyo-ní-zee) = it is snowing / το χιόνι = the snow φυσάει (fee-sá-ee) = it is windy / ο αέρας = the wind ο ήλιος (o eé-lyos) = the sun έχει ζέστη (é-hee zés-tee) = it's hot (literally: "it has heat") έχει κρύο (é-hee krí-o) = it's cold 3. The "Είναι + να" Expressions είναι κρίμα... (eé-ne krí-ma) = it's a pity... είναι λογικό... (eé-ne lo-yee-kó) = it makes sense... είναι δύσκολο / εύκολο... (dís-ko-lo / éf-ko-lo) = it is difficult / easy... Practice Grammar: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs

vocabulary-irregular-everyday.title: Irregular Nouns & Verbs in the Wild
vocabulary-irregular-everyday.content: C2 Vocabulary: Irregular Nouns & Verbs in the Wild To achieve true mastery, you must know the common words that completely break the standard grammatical rules you learned in A1-B2. 1. The "Ancient" Plurals (Irregular Neuter Nouns) Some neuter everyday words take an extra syllable (-ακ-) when pluralized or put in the genitive. το κρέας (to kré-as) = the meat ➔ Plural: τα κρέατα το τέρας (to té-ras) = the monster ➔ Plural: τα τέρατα το φως (to fos) = the light ➔ Plural: τα φώτα / Genitive: του φωτός 2. Unchanging Foreign Words These words, heavily used in daily life, never change their endings no matter the case or number. The only thing that changes is the article (ο, η, το)! το σπορ (to spor) = the sport ➔ τα σπορ (the sports) το κομπιούτερ (to kom-byoó-ter) = the computer ➔ τα κομπιούτερ το μπαρ (to bar) = the bar το ταξί (to ta-xí) = the taxi (Grammatically foreign words don't change, e.g. τα ταξί , but colloquially you will often hear native speakers say τα ταξιά !) 3. The Rebellious Adjectives The adjective "much/many" and "big" are famously tricky to decline. Learn these key forms: πολύς, πολλή, πολύ (po-lís) = much / many Plural Forms: πολλοί (masc), πολλές (fem), πολλά (neut) μέγας, μεγάλη, μέγα (mé-gas) = great / huge (from ancient Greek, now used alongside 'μεγάλος') 4. The 3rd Person "Frozen" Genitive Absolute Sometimes, Greeks use ancient participle constructions that seem disconnected from the sentence (Genitive Absolute). όντως (ón-dos) = indeed / in reality / factually όντος (ón-dos) = being (Ancient genitive participle) (e.g. όλων των πραγμάτων όντων... = all things being...) τρέχοντος (μήνα) (tré-hon-dos) = of the current (month)

vocabulary-particles-fillers.title: Conversational Fillers & Particles
vocabulary-particles-fillers.content: B2 Vocabulary: Conversational Fillers & Particles The little words that make you sound like a native. 1. The Greek "Um" and "Well" λοιπόν (lee-pón) = well... / so... δηλαδή (dee-la-dée) = meaning... / that is (to say)... βασικά (va-see-ká) = basically / actually ούτως ή άλλως (oó-tos ee ál-los) = anyway / either way 2. Expressing Doubt & Sarcasm άραγε (á-ra-ge) = I wonder... (used in questions) δήθεν / τάχα (dee-then / tá-ha) = supposedly / "yeah, right" μάλλον (mál-lon) = probably / I guess ίσως (í-sos) = perhaps / maybe 3. Strong Emotion & Wishes μακάρι (ma-ká-ree) = I hope / if only! ας (as) = let's / let it be ( ας πάμε = let's go) άντε / άιντε (án-de) = come on! / hurry up! αλίμονο (a-lí-mo-no) = alas / of course! 4. Agreement & Disagreement ακριβώς! (a-kree-vós) = exactly! φυσικά / βεβαίως (fee-see-ká / ve-vé-os) = naturally / certainly αποκλείεται! (a-po-klí-e-te) = no way! / impossible! Practice Grammar: Unit 22: Spoken Expressions

vocabulary-questions.title: Question Words & Pronouns
vocabulary-questions.content: A1 Vocabulary: Question Words & Pronouns Mastering how to ask for things. 1. The "W" Words Τι; (ti) = What? Πού; (poo) = Where? Πότε; (pó-te) = When? Πώς; (pos) = How? Γιατί; (ya-tí) = Why? 2. Chameleon Question Words Ποιος / Ποια / Ποιο; (pyos / pya / pyo) = Who? / Which? Πόσο; (pó-so) = How much? Πόσα; (pó-sa) = How many? 3. Pronouns & Particles εγώ (e-gó) = I εσύ (e-sí) = you (singular) αυτός / αυτή / αυτό (af-tós / af-tí / af-tó) = he / she / it εμείς (e-mís) = we εσείς (e-sís) = you (plural) δεν (den) = not Practice Grammar: Unit 7: Asking Questions

vocabulary-routines-hobbies.title: Routines & B-Group Verbs
vocabulary-routines-hobbies.content: A2 Vocabulary: Routines & B-Group Verbs Common Type B verbs (B1 & B2 "singers") and expressions used to describe habits and routines. 1. Daily Routine Verbs (B1 -άω) These verbs love the Alpha sound. Remember: use them for habits (Imperfect/Continuous)! ξυπνάω (xip-ná-o) = to wake up μιλάω (mee-lá-o) = to speak / talk φοράω (fo-rá-o) = to wear (clothes) πεινάω (pee-ná-o) = to be hungry διψάω (deep-sá-o) = to be thirsty αγαπάω (a-ga-pá-o) = to love 2. Essential B2 Verbs (-ώ) These are crucial verbs for requesting and giving permission. μπορώ (bo-ró) = to be able to / can ζω (zo) = to live τηλεφωνώ (tee-le-fo-nó) = to call on the phone λειτουργώ (lee-toor-gó) = to function / operate 3. Routine Nouns Combine these with verbs to describe your day. το πρωί (to pro-í) = the morning το μεσημέρι (to me-see-mé-ree) = noon / midday το απόγευμα (to a-pó-yev-ma) = the afternoon το βράδυ (to vrá-dee) = the evening / night η δουλειά (ee doo-lyá) = work / job Practice Grammar: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2

vocabulary-shopping-clothes.title: Shopping & Clothes
vocabulary-shopping-clothes.content: A2 Vocabulary: Shopping & Clothes Perfect for practicing plurals, adjective agreement, and object pronouns ("I want those ", "Give me it "). 1. Shopping Locations το μαγαζί / το κατάστημα (to ma-ga-zí / to ka-tá-stee-ma) = the shop / store η αγορά (ee a-go-rá) = the market το σούπερ μάρκετ (to soo-per már-ket) = the supermarket 2. Verbs for Shopping αγοράζω (a-go-rá-zo) = to buy (Simple Stem: αγοράσ-) πουλάω (poo-lá-o) = to sell (Type B1 verb) κοστίζει (ko-stí-zee) = it costs ψάχνω (psáh-no) = to look for / to search 3. Clothes (Τα ρούχα) Notice how many clothes are naturally plural, just like in English! τα ρούχα (ta roo-ha) = the clothes το παντελόνι (to pan-de-ló-nee) = the trousers / pants (Plural: τα παντελόνια) το παπούτσι (to pa-poo-tsee) = the shoe (Plural: τα παπούτσια) η μπλούζα (ee bloo-za) = the blouse / t-shirt το φόρεμα (to fó-re-ma) = the dress το μπουφάν (to boo-fán) = the jacket 4. Money & Descriptors τα λεφτά / τα χρήματα (ta lef-tá / ta hrí-ma-ta) = the money ακριβός, -ή, -ό (ak-ree-vós) = expensive φθηνός, -ή, -ό (fthee-nós) = cheap μεγάλος, -η, -ο (me-gá-los) = big μικρός, -ή, -ό (mik-rós) = small Practice Grammar: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives

vocabulary-survival.title: Survival Phrases
vocabulary-survival.content: A1 Foundations: Survival Phrases In this first vocabulary list, we focus on the words that will help you survive your first 24 hours in Greece. 1. Greetings (Χαιρετισμοί) Γεια σου (ya soo) = Hello / Goodbye (informal) Γεια σας (ya sas) = Hello / Goodbye (formal or plural) Καλημέρα (ka-lee-mé-ra) = Good morning Καλησπέρα (ka-lee-spé-ra) = Good evening Καληνύχτα (ka-lee-née-hta) = Good night 2. Politeness (Ευγένεια) Ευχαριστώ (ef-ha-ree-stó) = Thank you Παρακαλώ (pa-ra-ka-ló) = Please / You're welcome Συγγνώμη (see-gnó-mee) = Sorry / Excuse me Ναι (ne) = Yes Όχι (o-hee) = No 3. Basic Needs (Βασικές Ανάγκες) το Νερό (to ne-ró) = Water το Φαγητό (to fa-yee-tó) = Food ο Λογαριασμός (o lo-ga-rya-smós) = The bill (in a restaurant) η Βοήθεια (ee vo-ée-thya) = Help Πού είναι...; (poo ee-ne) = Where is...? Practice Grammar: Unit 5: The Accusative Case

vocabulary-time-calendar.title: Time & Calendar
vocabulary-time-calendar.content: A2 Vocabulary: Time & Calendar Essential vocabulary for dealing with time, predicting the future, and talking about the past. Notice how these words pair perfectly with verbs in the Aorist or Future tense! 1. Days of the Week (Οι μέρες της εβδομάδας) Note: All days are feminine except for Saturday (το Σάββατο). η Δευτέρα (ee thef-té-ra) = Monday η Τρίτη (ee trí-tee) = Tuesday η Τετάρτη (ee te-tár-tee) = Wednesday η Πέμπτη (ee pém-ptee) = Thursday η Παρασκευή (ee pa-ra-ske-ví) = Friday το Σάββατο (to sáv-va-to) = Saturday η Κυριακή (ee kee-rya-kí) = Sunday 2. Time Expressions (Εκφράσεις χρόνου) σήμερα (sí-me-ra) = today αύριο (áv-ryo) = tomorrow (use with Simple Future: Θα πάω αύριο ) χθες / εχθές (hthes / e-hthés) = yesterday (use with Aorist: Πήγα χθες ) τώρα (tó-ra) = now μετά (me-tá) = later / after πριν (preen) = before 3. Frequency (for the "Video" Continuous verbs) πάντα (pán-da) = always συχνά (see-hná) = often κάθε μέρα (ká-the mé-ra) = every day ποτέ (po-té) = never (used with δεν : Δεν παίζω ποτέ = I never play) Practice Grammar: Unit 11: Future Tenses (Θα)

vocabulary-travel-experiences.title: Travel & Life Experiences
vocabulary-travel-experiences.content: B1 Vocabulary: Travel & Life Experiences These words are naturally used with the Perfect Tenses (Have you ever...?) and Conditionals (If I go...). 1. Life Experiences Verbs ταξιδεύω (ta-xi-dév-o) = to travel (Perfect: έχω ταξιδέψει ) επισκέπτομαι (e-pis-kép-to-me) = to visit (Perfect: έχω επισκεφτεί ) γνωρίζω (gno-rí-zo) = to meet / to know (Perfect: έχω γνωρίσει ) δοκιμάζω (do-kee-má-zo) = to try / to taste (Perfect: έχω δοκιμάσει ) 2. Travel Nouns το ταξίδι (to ta-xí-di) = the trip / journey η χώρα (ee hó-ra) = the country το εισιτήριο (to ee-see-tí-ree-o) = the ticket οι διακοπές (ee dya-ko-pés) = the holidays (always plural!) το νησί (to nee-sí) = the island η θάλασσα (ee thá-las-sa) = the sea 3. The "Experience" Adverbs όταν (ó-tan) = when (not for questions; used for "when I was there...") αν (an) = if (the key to Conditionals!) ήδη (í-dee) = already ( Το έχω ήδη κάνει - I have already done it) ακόμα (a-kó-ma) = yet / still ( Δεν το έχω δει ακόμα - I haven't seen it yet) Practice Grammar: Unit 20: The Perfect Tenses
